Jersey worn as an alternative to a home uniform or an away uniform during games

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/SOC-Football-third-2004.jpg/250px-SOC-Football-third-2004.jpg)](/wiki/File:SOC-Football-third-2004.jpg)

A [Scotland national football team](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") orange third jersey.

A **third jersey**, **alternative jersey**, **third kit**, **third sweater** or **alternative uniform** is a [team jersey](/wiki/Team_jersey "Team jersey") or [uniform](/wiki/Sports_uniform "Sports uniform") that a [sports team](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") can wear instead of its [home](/wiki/Home_(sports) "Home (sports)") outfit or its [away outfit](/wiki/Away_colours "Away colours") during games, often when the colors of two competing teams' other uniforms are too similar to contrast easily.

Alternative jerseys are a lucrative means for professional sports organizations to generate revenue, by sales to [fans](/wiki/Sports_fan "Sports fan"). Of North American sports leagues, the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") ([gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football")) generates $1.2 billion annually in jersey sales, with the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") second, selling $900 million annually.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Another use of the alternative uniform is for identifying with causes. For example, the [Central Coast Mariners](/wiki/Central_Coast_Mariners_FC "Central Coast Mariners FC") wear an alternative pink kit on [pink ribbon](/wiki/Pink_ribbon "Pink ribbon") day.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Extra alternative uniforms or **fourth** and **fifth kits** are not commonly used, but are sometimes required when teams' other uniforms cause color clashes, or the uniforms are unavailable to use. In cases where teams have worn more than three kits in the same season, the extra kits were usually recycled from previous seasons.

Third-choice jerseys or uniforms are used in all five [major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada").

Third [kits](/wiki/Kit_(association_football) "Kit (association football)") are commonplace in professional European [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and in some professional European [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") clubs. Alternative uniforms are common in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia")'s two biggest domestic leagues, the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") ([Aussie rules](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football")) and [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") ([rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league")).

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

As a general rule in most sports, the home team has the privilege to select its desired color first, and the visiting team must then choose a suitable contrasting color. The "third" jersey should be distinct from the "first" (home) and "second" (away) and thus a visiting team should always have at least one suitable alternative choice. The actual choice may be guided by tradition, but modern sports leagues often impose specific rules, especially in professional leagues.

For home and away jerseys in North America, historical convention has often dictated the colors used by teams in a given league. Teams generally have one jersey which is primarily in a team color, and another jersey which is primarily white (or another light color) and accented with a team color. "White at home" is the convention in baseball ([MLB](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")), basketball ([NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), [NCAA basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball"), and [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA")), minor league professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") ([AHL](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") and [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL")), and [college ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey"). "White while [away](/wiki/Away_colours "Away colours")" is the convention in football ([NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), [CFL](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League"), [NCAA football](/wiki/College_football "College football")), major league professional ice hockey ([NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")), and professional [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse") ([NLL](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League") and [MLL](/wiki/Major_League_Lacrosse "Major League Lacrosse")). [Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") ([MLS](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer")) does not have a "white at home" or a "white while away" convention.

The NHL (and formerly the NBA) enforces the color/white rule strictly; any NHL team seeking to wear white at home must get express permission from the league office to do so. In minor league hockey, the rules are set in both the AHL and ECHL where the team wears white jerseys at home during one half of the season, then wears the color jerseys during the other half at home, and vice versa on the road.[[4]](#cite_note-sabreswhiteathome-4) In the NFL, the rules state that the home team has the first choice of color, with the [visiting team](/wiki/Road_(sports) "Road (sports)") forced to choose a contrasting color; an exception was [Color Rush](/wiki/Color_Rush "Color Rush"), in which uniform choices were coordinated by the league itself.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Starting with their uniform contract with [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") that begins with the [2017-2018 season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_NBA_season "2017–18 NBA season"), the NBA has abolished the color/white rule.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Instead, each team will designate whether their white uniform, now dubbed the "Association Edition," or their colored uniform, called the "Icon Edition," will be the home uniform, with the other becoming their designated away uniform.

In American sports, [throwback jerseys](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") are generally only used for special team games and not for the "third" purpose. In American football a third jersey may be a [throwback uniform](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") based on designs the team used in the past. In association football, meanwhile, it is more commonly a radically different design.

## American football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: American football")]

### National Football League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: National Football League")]

The [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") was the last of the major professional sports leagues to adopt the third jersey rule in 2002, with the only exceptions being the [1994 season](/wiki/1994_NFL_season "1994 NFL season"), when teams issued a [throwback uniform](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") in honour of the league's 75th Anniversary. Initially, the NFL rule stated that a team may wear their third jersey only once a year, however, after one year this restriction was increased to two times a year, and then three soon after. Some teams have exceeded the limit; the [2017 Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2017_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2017 Baltimore Ravens season") were an example, wearing their all-black uniforms twice that season, and the less-frequent black jerseys on white pants once. There are currently no rules on wearing alternative pants. Teams are only permitted to wear alternative jerseys once in playoff games (except the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), where teams must wear their standard uniforms); the only team to do so (other than in 1994) was the 2008 [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers"). In 2021, the Los Angeles Rams were permitted to wear their white alternate uniforms for Super Bowl.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the past, rules allowed for teams to wear their third jersey two times in the regular season and once in the preseason until 2010. In 2011 teams were no longer allowed to wear their third jersey in the preseason.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, there have been some exceptions since 2011. Beginning in 2018, the NFL began allowing teams to wear their alternates three times, and allowed two different alternates to be worn during the season.

Some teams will generally use one of their third jersey allotments against a particular division opponent each year. For instance, the Los Angeles (formerly San Diego) Chargers would frequently wear their popular alternate powder blue jerseys (that was introduced in 2007) at home against the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"), while the [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans") were known to wear their alternate "Battle Red" uniforms at home against the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars"), and the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") usually wore their [throwbacks](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") from 2007 to 2011 at home against the [archrival](/wiki/Steelers%E2%80%93Ravens_rivalry "Steelers–Ravens rivalry") [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens"). The [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") were known to wear their alternate red jerseys at home against the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") until the red jerseys were retired in 2009. The [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") have frequently worn their throwback uniform against the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") in recent years. The [Washington Commanders](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders") wear their alternative uniform on home games to commemorate their annual homecoming game once a year since 2012.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Tampabay_buccaneers_unif20.png/250px-Tampabay_buccaneers_unif20.png)](/wiki/File:Tampabay_buccaneers_unif20.png)

The uniforms of the Tampa Bay Buccaneers since the 2020 season, featuring a third dark grey one

When wearing their third jerseys, especially if the team is wearing a [throwback uniform](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform"), the team may theme the field around the uniforms. When the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), for instance, wore their 1960–1962 "Titans of New York" throwbacks at home, they painted the field in the Titans blue-and-gold color scheme (The Jets' current color scheme is green and white). In addition, the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") dressed the field up in orange when they wore their "Creamsicle" throwbacks in 2009.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Broncos_uniforms.png/250px-Broncos_uniforms.png)](/wiki/File:Broncos_uniforms.png)

Denver Broncos uniforms featuring a third navy blue and orange jersey, worn since 1997

Teams will generally wear their third jerseys at home, although clubs may wear them as the visiting team as well. The [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") are known to wear their electric blue jerseys on the road (most notably in Tampa) if the home team opts to wear their white jerseys against the Panthers and depending on the circumstance (primarily in warmer weather situations, where home teams will wear white to force the visiting team to have their uniforms absorb heat rather than reflect it). Since introducing a wolf grey alternate in [2012](/wiki/2012_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2012 Seattle Seahawks season"), the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") have worn their alternates only when on the road. The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos") wore their alternate navy blue jersey with blue pants in a game against the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") in 2013, and in 2017 the [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") also wore their alternate [powder blue](/wiki/Powder_blue "Powder blue") jersey against the Dallas Cowboys for a Thanksgiving Day matchup despite Dallas wearing navy blue. The [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") have worn their Titans throwback uniform once in 2007 against the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") at [Dolphin Stadium](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Stadium "Hard Rock Stadium") in Miami Gardens, and in 2017 the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") also wore their white throwback uniform against the Miami Dolphins in a week 17 meeting when Miami was also wearing their throwbacks. The Tennessee Titans, who switched back to navy as the primary color in 2015, wore their alternate [Columbia blue](/wiki/Columbia_blue "Columbia blue") jersey with white pants on the road in 2016, 2017, and 2018 when their home opponent forced them to wear their dark colors. The Las Vegas Raiders, who previously wore their white jersey with silver numbers for the color rush promotion from 2016 to 2017, wore the alternates for road games at San Francisco in 2018 and at the Los Angeles Chargers in 2020.

Because the [football helmet](/wiki/Football_helmet "Football helmet") is such a significant and visible part of the football uniform, some teams also wore alternate helmets when wearing their third jersey. This was particularly true for throwback uniforms, such as those worn by the [Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") (white), [Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") (white), [Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") (yellow), [Jets](/wiki/NY_Jets "NY Jets")/[Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") (blue), and [Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Buccaneers "Tampa Buccaneers") (white). The NFL outlawed the use of alternate helmets beginning in the 2013 season. This has had impact on some teams wanting to wear their throwback jerseys while other teams are not affected by the limitation. [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") owner [Jeffrey Lurie](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lurie "Jeffrey Lurie") has tried to persuade the league to reverse its one-helmet policy, to allow the Eagles to wear matching [kelly green](/wiki/Kelly_green "Kelly green") helmets and jerseys.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) However, beginning in 2022, teams will be allowed to wear an alternate helmet if they so choose.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Outside of league-wide promotions, the only team to not have had an official third uniform as of the [2025](/wiki/2025_NFL_season "2025 NFL season") season are the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs").

#### Color Rush

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Color Rush")]

Main article: [NFL Color Rush](/wiki/NFL_Color_Rush "NFL Color Rush")

For the 2015 season, the NFL debuted a "Color Rush" jersey concept for select Thursday games (*[Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")* and one [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") game) in celebration of 50 years of games being broadcast in color. The games featured, mostly, both teams wearing one color matching jerseys, pants, socks and shoes, specially designed to clash with the color of the other team.

The games were as follows:

* November 12: [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") (Red) at [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") (Kelly Green)
* November 19: [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") (Titans Blue) at [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") (Bold Gold),
* November 26: [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") (Panthers Blue) at [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") (White, due to [superstitions about wearing colored jerseys](/wiki/Away_colours#White_at_home,_colour_away "Away colours")),
* December 17: [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") (Red) at [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") (Yellow Gold).[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

For 2016, all NFL teams had Color Rush uniforms, although nine teams did not wear them on the field during the 2016 season.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The 2017 season also featured Color Rush games, but Washington (who wore all burgundy combo) and Cleveland did not wear their color rush uniforms. The program as a whole is no longer required prior to the 2018 season; teams have the option of whether or not they want to wear their color rush uniforms for the Thursday night games and can wear them beyond that point.

#### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Starting in 2025, the NFL introduced a "Rivalries" uniform program where special uniforms were worn specifically for games against division opponents. The plan is for teams to wear these uniforms at one home divisional game per season. For the 2025 season, teams in the [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") and [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") were the first to wear the new "Rivalries" uniform, followed by the [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") and [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") in 2026, the [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") and [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") in 2027, and the [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") and [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South") in 2028.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)

### College gridiron football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: College gridiron football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/North_carolina_football_unif17.png/250px-North_carolina_football_unif17.png)](/wiki/File:North_carolina_football_unif17.png)

[North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_football "North Carolina Tar Heels football") uniforms for the 2017 season, with a third jersey in black

Uniforms are much less regulated at the [collegiate level](/wiki/College_football "College football") compared to the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL"). Tradition continues to guide many teams, especially those with a history of sustained success or championships.

Since the late 2000's, many teams have begun to experiment with alternate uniforms and helmets. Black or dark gray jerseys (with a team color accent), and sometimes all-black uniforms, have been widely used; all-white uniforms are also popular.

[Ole Miss](/wiki/Ole_Miss_Rebels_football "Ole Miss Rebels football") was one of the earliest programs to use two different jerseys, wearing either navy blue or red jerseys depending upon the game.

The [University of Oregon Ducks](/wiki/Oregon_Ducks_football "Oregon Ducks football") have a modular uniform system, consisting of as many as four color choices for jerseys, pants, helmets, socks and other components, allowing the Ducks to select a new combination for nearly every game.

One of the more famous third jerseys is that of the [University of Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football"). The team wears either white or navy blue for most games, but occasionally special kelly green jerseys with gold numbers, evocative of the "Fighting Irish", are chosen for a major contest.

## Association football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Association football")]

Third kits traditionally started in European competition as a way of marking the occasion and existed in association football at least as early as the 1930s. Until 1989–90,[[20]](#cite_note-HFK2011-20) the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") competition rules stated: "Where the colours of the two competing clubs are similar, both clubs must change unless alternative arrangements are mutually agreed by the competing clubs".[[21]](#cite_note-21) Away kits were often similar as well, therefore third kits were worn in the [1948 FA Cup Final](/wiki/1948_FA_Cup_Final "1948 FA Cup Final") by [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") and the [1950 final](/wiki/1950_FA_Cup_Final "1950 FA Cup Final") by [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.").[[22]](#cite_note-umbro1-22) Similar rules were employed by European governing body [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"), with Manchester United winning the [1968 European Cup Final](/wiki/1968_European_Cup_Final "1968 European Cup Final") in a blue third kit.[[22]](#cite_note-umbro1-22)

Teams sometimes needed to find makeshift third kits for their players.[[20]](#cite_note-HFK2011-20) One notable example being [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") having to wear the green-and-white stripes of local club [C.A. Kimberley](/wiki/Kimberley_de_Mar_del_Plata "Kimberley de Mar del Plata") for their [1978 World Cup](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup") match against [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team"), as both teams were only equipped with their white change shirts for the game.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Third kits in international football are less common; both teams are typically advised to change colours in the event of a home kit clashing.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") prepared light blue third kits for the [1970](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup") and [1986 World Cups](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup"), which were only worn for the former,[[22]](#cite_note-umbro1-22) and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team") introduced a white third kit for the [2014 World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") at the insistence of FIFA.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Since the 2000s, most clubs in major European leagues have used a third kit, or had one in reserve. Replicas of the kits are usually sold at club merchandise shops. Sometimes, a previous season's away kit is retained if a clash arises. Clubs sometimes also assign a third kit as their secondary or even primary kit for certain competitions and vice versa.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

One notable incident occurred in 1996, when [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") changed into their blue and white third kit – mainly used as their cup away kit – at half-time, with manager [Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson") blaming the grey [away shirt](/wiki/Away_colours "Away colours") for several sub-par performances.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Ferguson commented, "The players couldn't pick each other out.[...] They said it was difficult to see their team-mates at distance when they lifted their heads".[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") had to wear its [sky blue](/wiki/Sky_blue "Sky blue") training kit during a 2022 match against [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C."), because its home kit (black and white), away kit (navy blue) and third kit (white) were all deemed too similar to the blue and white home kit of Brighton & Hove Albion.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Baseball")]

### Major League Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Major League Baseball")]

See also: [Major League Baseball uniforms](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_uniforms "Major League Baseball uniforms")

After decades of wearing the same [uniforms](/wiki/Baseball_uniform "Baseball uniform"), [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball#MLB_uniforms "Major League Baseball") teams began to experiment with numerous designs in the early 1970s, ranging from popular designs (such as the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics")' pullover jerseys that most MLB teams later adopted) and not so popular (such as the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") wearing shorts, or the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") wearing all red).

Among such designs that were also tested were third jerseys, to break the traditional mold that baseball teams wear white uniforms at home, and gray on the road. This began in 1972 when the A's flamboyant owner, [Charles O. Finley](/wiki/Charlie_Finley "Charlie Finley"), introduced new uniforms to the team. Taking after the lead of the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates"), the jerseys were pullover [spandex](/wiki/Spandex "Spandex") that would later catch on in MLB (though this would be phased out by the early 1990s in favor of the more traditional button-down jerseys), but by going one step further than the Pirates, the A's introduced alternate gold and green jerseys. The gold jerseys, lighter in color, were considered "home" alternates while the darker green jerseys were considered "away" alternates.

Soon, many teams caught on with different colored jerseys. The Pirates even went as far as to having a rotation of which jerseys to wear, matching white pinstriped, gold, and black pants to wear with jerseys of the same color. The white [pinstripes](/wiki/Pin_stripes "Pin stripes") were later phased out in favor of solid white.

The [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") have generally shunned the practice of third jerseys. The Yankees wore three different jerseys in 1911, 1916, and most recently in 1943 according to the Dressed to the Nines database maintained by the National Baseball Hall of Fame. In recent years, the Yankees have worn [throwback uniforms](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") for single games in 1996 and 2012.[[30]](#cite_note-yankees-alt-30)

On May 7, 1995, the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") wore an alternate navy jersey with a tiger stepping through a white old English "D" as their logo. At the time, it was the only game that the Tigers wore a third jersey. However, starting in 2026, the Tigers added an orange home alternate jersey and a navy blue road alternate jersey. This leaves the Yankees as the only team without an official third jersey.

Third jerseys otherwise remain popular in baseball today, either as an alternate design or as a throwback known as "Turn Back the Clock Night." The [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers"), for example, have worn a variation of their 1978–1993 home uniforms for every Friday home game. In 1999, the Seattle Mariners hosted the Kansas City Royals for a game where both teams wore "futuristic" uniforms meant to represent the year 2027 (what will be the Mariners 50th season). The "[Turn Ahead The Clock](/wiki/Turn_Ahead_The_Clock "Turn Ahead The Clock")" promotion was so successful that it was copied the next year by 20 MLB teams, this time representing the year 2021 (due to sponsorship by the real estate company [Century 21](/wiki/Century_21_Real_Estate "Century 21 Real Estate")); however, the jerseys were roundly ridiculed and have not been seen since.

The [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") change from their traditional blue and grey jersey to a red alternate jersey every [Canada Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day") (July 1) to help celebrate the national holiday (and with it, a cap with a red maple leaf on it, which is Canada's national symbol). In 2007, the Jays announced that as part of the team's popular "Flashback Friday" promotion, the team would use replica uniforms based on the powder-blue road uniforms used in the 1980s at all Friday night home games, starting with the 2008 season. To complete the look, the Jays also wore the original blue and white [caps](/wiki/Baseball_cap "Baseball cap"), with their traditional logo on the front of the uniform as well as on the caps. In 2017, to celebrate Canada's 150th anniversary, the Blue Jays wore alternate red jerseys and caps for every Sunday home game.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays") wore a black alternate from 1998 to 2000 when they were the Devil Rays, and from 2005 to 2007 they wore green alternate jersey worn both at home and away on selected games. When they changed their name to the Rays, they introduced an alternate navy blue for home and away games to go along with their white and gray uniforms. In 2010 they added light blue jerseys to be worn only on Sunday home games. The [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") wear a red alternate at many afternoon home games, and the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") wear a red jersey for every home game played on Sunday, while the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals") wear red for all weekend home games, and in 2011 have done so for most weekend road games as well. The [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") wore a red alternate for every home game played on Friday during the [2007 season](/wiki/2007_in_baseball "2007 in baseball"), even though red is not an official team color. Since [2009](/wiki/2009_Major_League_Baseball_season "2009 Major League Baseball season"), the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") wore a red alternate jersey for Friday night home games and a blue alternate jersey for Friday night away games. Also since 2008, for every afternoon home game, the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals") wear powder-blue jerseys almost reminiscent of the old jerseys they wore in the 1980s (and in 2010 introduced new powder-blue caps to be worn with these jerseys, though they have since gone back to wearing their normal blue caps). The [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") wear orange jerseys during all Friday home games. The [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") introduced an alternate cream-colored uniform (with a blue cap to complete the look) in 2008 and currently wear it for all afternoon home games. Most recently the Mariners revived their teal jerseys from the mid-1990s, to be used on Friday home games. The [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") also have a navy blue alternate away jersey. In 2015 they also introduced an alternate version of their home uniforms, using the team's classic blue-and-yellow scheme on cream uniforms, to be worn on Sunday home games. The [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles") wear alternate black jerseys (with a cap showing the "O's" script logo) every Friday, regardless of whether they are home or away (home games against the Red Sox would force the latter to wear their regular grey uniforms rather than their navy Friday uniforms), and in 2012 they introduced an alternate orange jersey to be worn during Saturday games.

In 2013, the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") introduced two different blue alternate jerseys; one has their team name on it (in orange lettering) and is used at home, while the other has their city name on it (in gray lettering) and is used on the road. Prior to that, the Mets wore two black alternate jerseys in a similar fashion as their current blue alternates.

To honor the U.S. military, during Sunday home games, the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") originally sported special [camouflage](/wiki/Camouflage "Camouflage")-colored jerseys (with green caps to match); in 2011 the camouflage jerseys were changed to a brown/tan "desert camo" with a tan cap. Then in 2016, the Padres replaced them with a new "navy camo" jersey (honoring the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy")) with a blue cap. Also, they released a modernized version of the franchise's classic brown jerseys with brown-and-yellow caps (which was originally worn from 1969 to 1984) to be worn for Friday home games.

In contrast, the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") frequently wear a blue alternate jersey on the road, and not according to the day of the week. This jersey is worn based on the decision of the [starting pitcher](/wiki/Starting_pitcher "Starting pitcher") (particularly when [Carlos Zambrano](/wiki/Carlos_Zambrano "Carlos Zambrano") was scheduled to start), thus explaining why it is worn more often than other third jerseys.

After the death of [Harmon Killebrew](/wiki/Harmon_Killebrew "Harmon Killebrew") in 2011, the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins") decided to wear their cream-colored "throwback" third jersey – a direct replica of the uniforms used for most of Killebrew's career – for every home game for the rest of the year.

The [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") have two alternate uniforms with the same color, with one featuring the team nickname and used on home games, the other featuring the city name and used on away games; in addition to their regular alternates, since 2011 the Brewers have, during various "Heritage" games, worn uniforms with "*Cerveceros*" ("Brewers" in Spanish), "*Bierbrauer*" (German), "*Birrai*" (Italian) and "*Piwowarzy*" (Polish); their opponents during those games would usually also have the foreign translation of their own team name on their road uniforms, such as "*Piraten*" ("Pirates" in German) or "*Cardenales*" ("Cardinals" in Spanish).

More recently, there is also a trend towards alternate grey uniforms. The [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") wear a different version of their road grey uniforms, featuring the interlocking "SF" instead of the city name in full. This design is similar to the road uniforms the team utilized in the late 80's. The Dodgers and the Cubs have followed suit in wearing alternate grey uniforms, sporting the team name in front (rather than the city name) for their grey alternates.

All 30 MLB teams wore special colored jerseys with contrasting sleeves on the weekend of August 25–27, 2017, for [Players Weekend](/wiki/Players_Weekend "Players Weekend"). The Cardinals and Pirates also used these jerseys on August 20, when they met in the MLB Little League Game in [Williamsport, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Williamsport,_Pennsylvania "Williamsport, Pennsylvania"); both this game and Players Weekend coincided with the [2017 Little League World Series](/wiki/2017_Little_League_World_Series "2017 Little League World Series") in [South Williamsport](/wiki/South_Williamsport,_Pennsylvania "South Williamsport, Pennsylvania"). Notably, the 2017 Players Weekend marked the first time the Yankees ever placed names on the back of their jerseys.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Players Weekend has since become an annual affair, with similar jersey changes for each subsequent event.

The trend toward multiple uniforms of the same color can also lead to on-field mix-ups: in 2011, Brewers pitcher [Zack Greinke](/wiki/Zack_Greinke "Zack Greinke"), making a pinch-hitting appearance, accidentally wore the "*Bierbrauer*" jersey the night before German Heritage Day,[[33]](#cite_note-33) while in 2014, just three games into the first season where the alternate was introduced, outfielder [Junior Lake](/wiki/Junior_Lake "Junior Lake") played the first inning in a "*Chicago*" jersey while the rest of the team wore "*Cubs*".[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 2023, Major League Baseball limited teams to two alternate uniforms in addition to a home and road uniform and a City Connect uniform; this is known as the "4+1 rule".[[35]](#cite_note-Nike4plus1uniforms-35) While some teams complied with the rule, including teams such as the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") and [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays") replacing their gray road uniforms with colored tops, MLB allowed the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") to keep their two Sunday camouflage home uniforms in order to maintain their tradition of honoring the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces"). In addition to the two aforementioned uniforms, the Padres also wear brown tops with sand pants as an alternate road uniform.
