## Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Basketball")]

### National Basketball Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: National Basketball Association")]

The concept of the third jersey in the NBA was first introduced when the [Atlanta Hawks](/wiki/Atlanta_Hawks "Atlanta Hawks") wore neon green alternate jerseys in addition to their standard blue road and white home jerseys during the [1970–71](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_NBA_season "1970–71 NBA season") and [1971–72](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_NBA_season "1971–72 NBA season") seasons. However, it was not until the mid-1990s that third jerseys became a common trend throughout the league.

The [1994–95](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NBA_season "1994–95 NBA season") season brought the first wave of third jerseys in the NBA, as the Hawks, [Charlotte Hornets](/wiki/Charlotte_Hornets "Charlotte Hornets"), [Detroit Pistons](/wiki/Detroit_Pistons "Detroit Pistons"), [Orlando Magic](/wiki/Orlando_Magic "Orlando Magic"), [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") and [Sacramento Kings](/wiki/Sacramento_Kings "Sacramento Kings") released new alternate uniforms. The trend continued in the [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_NBA_season "1995–96 NBA season") season with the [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls"), [Miami Heat](/wiki/Miami_Heat "Miami Heat"), [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") and [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") introducing their own third jerseys.

Since then, third jerseys became a regular part of every NBA team's uniform rotation, but at first, not all teams were receptive with the concept. For instance, the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") and [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") initially refused to join the trend out of respect to tradition, but they eventually unveiled new alternates in the [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NBA_season "2002–03 NBA season") and [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NBA_season "2005–06 NBA season") seasons respectively.

The third jersey trend only got stronger when the NBA signed exclusive uniform contracts with [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") and later [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"). The Adidas deal, in particular, saw the introduction of additional alternate uniforms in an effort to boost jersey sales; examples include the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors")' sleeved alternates and the Heat's monochrome uniforms.

In addition to third jerseys, there are also holiday-themed jerseys that are worn only on special occasions. These include the jerseys worn on [Christmas Day](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association_Christmas_games "National Basketball Association Christmas games"), [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year"), [Mardi Gras](/wiki/New_Orleans_Mardi_Gras "New Orleans Mardi Gras") (for the [New Orleans Pelicans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Pelicans "New Orleans Pelicans")), and [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day_in_the_United_States#Basketball "Saint Patrick's Day in the United States").

Other alternate uniforms in the NBA include the "Noche Latina" uniforms, military-inspired uniforms, and "Pride" uniforms, all of which were also concepts introduced by Adidas.

NBA teams were only allowed to introduce a new third jersey at least two years after unveiling a new logo and uniform set. This rule has since been relaxed somewhat, following more recent third jersey releases by the [Utah Jazz](/wiki/Utah_Jazz "Utah Jazz"), the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets"), the [New Orleans Pelicans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Pelicans "New Orleans Pelicans"), the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks"), the [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers"), the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") the [Charlotte Hornets](/wiki/Charlotte_Hornets "Charlotte Hornets") and the [Atlanta Hawks](/wiki/Atlanta_Hawks "Atlanta Hawks"), in which some of the teams release their alternates on the same day as their regular home and away set.

Teams are also allowed to wear their third jerseys as often as desired during the first three rounds of the [NBA Playoffs](/wiki/NBA_Playoffs "NBA Playoffs") and the [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals"). Per the NBA Rule Book, there is no official rule that states that teams are restricted on wearing alternate uniforms or white at home during the NBA Finals. They only requirement stated is from Section VI, c. which states, "The home team shall wear light color jerseys, and the visitors dark jerseys unless otherwise approved. For neutral court games and doubleheaders, the second team named in the official schedule shall be regarded as the home team and shall wear the light colored jerseys." An example of this was when the [Cleveland Cavaliers](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavaliers "Cleveland Cavaliers") won the [2016 NBA Finals](/wiki/2016_NBA_Finals "2016 NBA Finals") in a black sleeved alternate jersey.

This rule concerning uniform designations was eliminated once [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc "Nike, Inc") became the uniform provider for the NBA in 2017. In doing so, teams now have the freedom to choose whichever uniform they want to wear, whether it's the "Association" white jersey (formerly the de facto home jersey), the "Icon" dark jersey (formerly the de facto away jersey), the "Statement" alternate color jersey (formerly the de facto third jersey) and the annual "City" and (in 2019 and 2021) "Earned" jerseys. The "Statement" jerseys are usually changed after a three-year cycle, whether through a minor alteration or a major overhaul. Like with Nike's later uniform contract with Major League Baseball, its contract with the NBA only limited teams to three "core" uniforms plus an annual "City" jersey and an occasional "Classic" jersey, leading to the disappearance of limited edition holiday and Pride-themed uniforms from the Adidas era.

## Canadian football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Canadian football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Geroy_Simon_2011.jpg/250px-Geroy_Simon_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geroy_Simon_2011.jpg)

[Geroy Simon](/wiki/Geroy_Simon "Geroy Simon") wearing the [BC Lions](/wiki/BC_Lions "BC Lions")' black third jersey in [2011](/wiki/2011_BC_Lions_season "2011 BC Lions season").

The [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") first introduced the use of a permanent third jersey on September 6, 1994, when the [Calgary Stampeders](/wiki/Calgary_Stampeders "Calgary Stampeders") wore black uniforms for the [Labour Day Classic](/wiki/Labour_Day_Classic "Labour Day Classic") against the [Edmonton Eskimos](/wiki/Edmonton_Eskimos "Edmonton Eskimos").[[37]](#cite_note-Whatever-37) The CFL did not have a rule for use of alternate uniforms and teams were able to wear them as often or as little as they liked, a rule that is still in effect. Teams are also able to wear multiple alternate jerseys within the same season, as seen with the [Montreal Alouettes](/wiki/Montreal_Alouettes "Montreal Alouettes") during their [2009 campaign](/wiki/2009_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2009 Montreal Alouettes season") when they wore alternate black, blue, and [throwback jerseys](/wiki/Throwback_jersey "Throwback jersey"), in addition to their regular home and away uniforms. Teams are also permitted to wear their third jerseys as the away team, provided that they do not clash with the home team's uniforms. Despite teams having no restrictions with third jerseys, only the Stampeders chose to wear them during the 1990s, presumably due to the league's financial struggles during this decade.[[38]](#cite_note-38) As such, no [CFL USA](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League_in_the_United_States "Canadian Football League in the United States") team ever wore an alternate uniform. The Alouettes were the next team to introduce third uniforms in [2001](/wiki/2001_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2001 Montreal Alouettes season") when they also wore black uniforms; a colour that was not part of the team's colour scheme. The [Saskatchewan Roughriders](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Roughriders "Saskatchewan Roughriders") first wore their black alternates in [2002](/wiki/2002_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2002 Saskatchewan Roughriders season") as it became more normal for teams to wear these jerseys. The [BC Lions](/wiki/BC_Lions "BC Lions") wore orange retro uniforms twice during the [2003 season](/wiki/2003_BC_Lions_season "2003 BC Lions season") as part of the celebrations for their 50th season, but those were only worn for that season.

In the [2005 CFL season](/wiki/2005_CFL_season "2005 CFL season"), [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") redesigned the primary home and away uniforms for all nine teams and also introduced new alternate uniforms for six teams, with the [Hamilton Tiger-Cats](/wiki/Hamilton_Tiger-Cats "Hamilton Tiger-Cats") and [Toronto Argonauts](/wiki/Toronto_Argonauts "Toronto Argonauts") abstaining and the [Saskatchewan Roughriders](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Roughriders "Saskatchewan Roughriders") keeping their alternates that they had been using since 2002.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Calgary introduced the third iteration of their black uniforms while the Alouettes wore a blue jersey with red numerals.[[40]](#cite_note-2005_Stampeders-40) The [BC Lions](/wiki/BC_Lions "BC Lions"), having now converted to orange as the primary jersey colour, wore black alternates, the [Edmonton Eskimos](/wiki/Edmonton_Eskimos "Edmonton Eskimos") and [Winnipeg Blue Bombers](/wiki/Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers "Winnipeg Blue Bombers") wore their own versions of gold alternates, and the [Ottawa Renegades](/wiki/Ottawa_Renegades "Ottawa Renegades") wore red alternate jerseys.

For [2006](/wiki/2006_CFL_season "2006 CFL season"), the Tiger-Cats introduced gold alternate jerseys on August 12, 2006, in a game against the Argonauts.[[41]](#cite_note-cfl.ca-41) This season also saw the first case of both teams wearing alternate uniforms as the Alouettes brought back their black uniforms in a home game against the Eskimos who wore their gold jerseys.[[42]](#cite_note-Golden_moment_for_Goldie-42) The Winnipeg Blue Bombers also wore their alternate gold jerseys as the away team for their playoff game against the host Toronto Argonauts.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The Toronto Argonauts were the last CFL team to wear a third jersey as they wore alternate Cambridge blue uniforms on July 26, 2007.[[44]](#cite_note-argonauts.ca-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The league recognized the history of its teams by having member clubs wear [throwback uniforms](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") beginning with the [2008 CFL season](/wiki/2008_CFL_season "2008 CFL season") with the Toronto Argonauts and Winnipeg Blue Bombers wearing 1950s-style uniforms for two games featuring both teams.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The CFL then had all eight current teams wear 1960s-based uniforms for the [2009 CFL season](/wiki/2009_CFL_season "2009 CFL season") and 1970s-based uniforms for the [2010 CFL season](/wiki/2010_CFL_season "2010 CFL season").[[47]](#cite_note-47) These differed from the designated third jerseys in that most were for one season only, except for the Saskatchewan Roughriders white retro jersey and the Winnipeg Blue Bombers royal blue retro jersey that were worn in subsequent seasons. These throwback uniforms were worn to lead up to the [100th Grey Cup](/wiki/100th_Grey_Cup "100th Grey Cup") celebration.

Following the league-based retro initiative, the league then turned its attention to a modernized take on all nine teams (including the expansion [Ottawa Redblacks](/wiki/Ottawa_Redblacks "Ottawa Redblacks")) by introducing Signature uniforms mostly in the [2014 season](/wiki/2014_CFL_season##Signature_series "2014 CFL season").[[48]](#cite_note-Signature-48) The Lions first wore their Signature uniforms in [2013](/wiki/2013_BC_Lions_season "2013 BC Lions season") as part of their 60th season celebrations with the other eight teams following suit the following season. The uniforms were worn twice by each team except for Ottawa who wore theirs three times and Winnipeg who wore theirs once. In [2015](/wiki/2015_CFL_season "2015 CFL season"), Winnipeg abandoned the uniforms altogether, as did the BC Lions, despite their popularity in BC. Every other team wore theirs twice during 2015 except for Hamilton, who wore theirs once on [Labour Day](/wiki/Labour_Day_Classic "Labour Day Classic").

With the league-wide redesign of uniforms by [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") in [2016](/wiki/2016_CFL_season "2016 CFL season"), Calgary, Edmonton, and Montreal each retained their signature series uniforms and each wore them twice during the regular season (Calgary also wore theirs in the West Final game). Saskatchewan also wore a modified version of their retro-themed uniforms in two games that year. When [New Era](/wiki/New_Era_Cap_Company "New Era Cap Company") became the league outfitter in [2019](/wiki/2019_CFL_season "2019 CFL season"), only Calgary, Edmonton, and Saskatchewan wore third jerseys and all three were holdovers from the previous three seasons.[[49]](#cite_note-New_Era-49) Ottawa introduced new alternates in 2021 and four teams wore new alternates in 2022.

As of [2018](/wiki/2018_CFL_season "2018 CFL season"), teams are permitted to wear third jerseys during the playoffs, but not for the [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup") game.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The [2010 Saskatchewan Roughriders](/wiki/2010_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2010 Saskatchewan Roughriders season") were the last team to wear alternate uniforms for the championship game.

The following list is a recount of all known alternate uniforms worn by member clubs as of the [2024 CFL season](/wiki/2024_CFL_season "2024 CFL season") and does not include one-time [throwback uniforms](/wiki/Throwback_uniforms "Throwback uniforms"):

* [BC Lions](/wiki/BC_Lions "BC Lions") – Introduced a black alternate uniform in [2005](/wiki/2005_BC_Lions_season "2005 BC Lions season") loosely based on the [1960s jersey](/wiki/1964_BC_Lions_season "1964 BC Lions season"), initially worn with white helmets. Switched to orange helmets with black uniform from [2006](/wiki/2006_BC_Lions_season "2006 BC Lions season") to [2008](/wiki/2008_BC_Lions_season "2008 BC Lions season"), including the [2006 West final](/wiki/94th_Grey_Cup#West_Final "94th Grey Cup") and [94th Grey Cup](/wiki/94th_Grey_Cup "94th Grey Cup") win. Worn with black retro helmets in [2009](/wiki/2009_BC_Lions_season "2009 BC Lions season") and [2010](/wiki/2010_BC_Lions_season "2010 BC Lions season") seasons. Retired the jerseys in [2011](/wiki/2011_BC_Lions_season "2011 BC Lions season") wearing white helmets, in the final game at [Empire Field](/wiki/Empire_Field "Empire Field"). Introduced gunmetal uniforms, first worn on August 17, 2013, and worn twice during the [2013](/wiki/2013_BC_Lions_season "2013 BC Lions season") and [2014](/wiki/2014_BC_Lions_season "2014 BC Lions season") seasons, including an away game in Toronto on August 17, 2014.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Lions did not wear a third jersey again until October 15, 2022, when the team wore a modified version of the gunmetal uniforms.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The team also wore these uniforms for their playoff game in [2022](/wiki/2022_BC_Lions_season "2022 BC Lions season").[[53]](#cite_note-53) The Lions wore a new uniform set in [2023](/wiki/2023_BC_Lions_season "2023 BC Lions season"), but did not wear a third jersey that year. In [2024](/wiki/2024_BC_Lions_season "2024 BC Lions season"), the Lions introduced a third iteration of the gunmetal uniforms with new logos which were worn twice in the regular season.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
* [Calgary Stampeders](/wiki/Calgary_Stampeders "Calgary Stampeders") – First to wear third uniforms when they introduced black alternates with red numerals in [1994](/wiki/1994_Calgary_Stampeders_season "1994 Calgary Stampeders season") on September 6 against Edmonton. They were the first team to wear alternates during a playoff game and the first to wear alternates during a [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup") game, which they did in the [86th Grey Cup](/wiki/86th_Grey_Cup "86th Grey Cup") in [1998](/wiki/1998_CFL_season "1998 CFL season").[[37]](#cite_note-Whatever-37) Those black jerseys were retired following the Grey Cup win and new black alternates with white numerals were unveiled in [1999](/wiki/1999_Calgary_Stampeders_season "1999 Calgary Stampeders season") that were worn until [2004](/wiki/2004_Calgary_Stampeders_season "2004 Calgary Stampeders season"). They first wore black pants with the black jerseys on October 18, 2002 at home vs. the Winnipeg Blue Bombers.[[40]](#cite_note-2005_Stampeders-40) The third version of black uniforms were worn on Labour Day in [2005](/wiki/2005_Calgary_Stampeders_season "2005 Calgary Stampeders season") and were last worn on September 2, 2013. [2011](/wiki/2011_Calgary_Stampeders_season "2011 Calgary Stampeders season") was the first time the Stampeders wore black helmets with the black uniforms.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The Signature series uniforms were first worn, also on Labour Day, for the [2014 season](/wiki/2014_Calgary_Stampeders_season "2014 Calgary Stampeders season") and again from 2015 to 2021. They were also worn for home playoff games from 2015 to 2018. The team introduced new black uniforms in [2022](/wiki/2022_Calgary_Stampeders_season "2022 Calgary Stampeders season") which were first worn for the Labour Day game that year and once more in the final home game in 2022, which was repeated in [2023](/wiki/2022_Calgary_Stampeders_season "2022 Calgary Stampeders season").[[56]](#cite_note-56) Since 1994, the Stampeders have worn black on Labour Day 20 times.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)
* [Edmonton Elks](/wiki/Edmonton_Elks "Edmonton Elks") – First wore their gold alternate jersey on September 9, 2005, against the Calgary Stampeders.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Wore the gold alternates against the Alouettes in [2006](/wiki/2006_Edmonton_Eskimos_season "2006 Edmonton Eskimos season") as the away team as the Alouettes also wore their alternate uniforms.[[42]](#cite_note-Golden_moment_for_Goldie-42) The club last wore these jerseys on September 7, 2007, again against the Stampeders.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The club had no third jersey after the retirement of the gold jersey until the [2014 season](/wiki/2014_Edmonton_Eskimos_season "2014 Edmonton Eskimos season") when they wore their Signature series green uniforms with the gold "EE" logo. This uniform had been worn at least once each season until the uniforms were rebranded in [2022](/wiki/2022_Edmonton_Elks_season "2022 Edmonton Elks season") where no alternate uniforms were worn that year. In [2024](/wiki/2024_Edmonton_Elks_season "2024 Edmonton Elks season"), the Elks introduced green alternate uniforms with green numerals that were worn with gold helmets and green pants, which were worn three times that year.[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* [Hamilton Tiger-Cats](/wiki/Hamilton_Tiger-Cats "Hamilton Tiger-Cats") – Introduced a gold alternate jersey on August 12, 2006 against the Toronto Argonauts.[[41]](#cite_note-cfl.ca-41) The jerseys were always paired with black helmets and black pants. They were worn three times during the [2007](/wiki/2007_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2007 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season") and [2008](/wiki/2008_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2008 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season") seasons and worn for the last time on October 31, 2009 and just once for the [2009](/wiki/2009_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2009 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season") season as the team wore retro jerseys that same year.[[62]](#cite_note-62) The team had no alternate jersey until the Signature series grey uniform was unveiled in [2014](/wiki/2014_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2014 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season"). The grey uniform was worn three times between 2014 and 2015 and no alternate uniform was worn from 2016 to 2021. The team introduced new steel grey uniforms in [2022](/wiki/2022_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2022 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season") which were worn twice that year and twice again in [2023](/wiki/2023_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2023 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season") and [2024](/wiki/2024_Hamilton_Tiger-Cats_season "2024 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season"), including once on the road in both seasons.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/2023%2C_07%2C_Cody_Fajardo_%282%29.jpg/250px-2023%2C_07%2C_Cody_Fajardo_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2023,_07,_Cody_Fajardo_(2).jpg)

[Cody Fajardo](/wiki/Cody_Fajardo "Cody Fajardo") wearing the [Montreal Alouettes](/wiki/Montreal_Alouettes "Montreal Alouettes")' red third jersey in [2023](/wiki/2023_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2023 Montreal Alouettes season").

* [Montreal Alouettes](/wiki/Montreal_Alouettes "Montreal Alouettes") – Began wearing an all-black uniform in [2001](/wiki/2001_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2001 Montreal Alouettes season") and continued wearing them until [2004](/wiki/2004_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2004 Montreal Alouettes season") and two more times in [2006](/wiki/2006_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2006 Montreal Alouettes season"). Introduced a blue alternate with red numerals in [2005](/wiki/2005_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2005 Montreal Alouettes season") and wore these jerseys in the [93rd Grey Cup](/wiki/93rd_Grey_Cup "93rd Grey Cup"). The blue alternates were worn again in 2006, [2009](/wiki/2009_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2009 Montreal Alouettes season") and [2010](/wiki/2010_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2010 Montreal Alouettes season"). The team introduced a new black jersey, this time with grey numerals as opposed to blue, for the [2007](/wiki/2007_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2007 Montreal Alouettes season") and wore this jersey five times during the regular season while wearing the primary home jersey four times. This jersey was worn until the 2010 season and was brought back one more time for the [2013 season](/wiki/2013_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2013 Montreal Alouettes season").[[64]](#cite_note-64) The team wore dark grey and blue Signature series uniforms for the [2014 season](/wiki/2014_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2014 Montreal Alouettes season") and wore them two times in each subsequent season until 2016.[[48]](#cite_note-Signature-48) The uniforms were worn just once in 2017 and 2018 and when the Alouettes re-branded in [2019](/wiki/2019_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2019 Montreal Alouettes season"), the team did not wear a third jersey. The Alouettes had planned to introduce a fan-designed third jersey for the 2021 season as part of their 75th anniversary celebrations, but this did not occur following a cancelled [2020 CFL season](/wiki/2020_CFL_season "2020 CFL season").[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) Instead, this uniform was revealed in [2023](/wiki/2023_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2023 Montreal Alouettes season") and was worn three times during the regular season and once in the post-season, including twice for away games, but just once in [2024](/wiki/2024_Montreal_Alouettes_season "2024 Montreal Alouettes season").[[67]](#cite_note-67)
* [Ottawa Rough Riders](/wiki/Ottawa_Rough_Riders "Ottawa Rough Riders") – Never wore a third jersey.
* [Ottawa Renegades](/wiki/Ottawa_Renegades "Ottawa Renegades") – Introduced a red alternate jersey worn with white pants on July 1, 2005 against the Montreal Alouettes. Worn four times for the [2005 season](/wiki/2005_Ottawa_Renegades_season "2005 Ottawa Renegades season") including a game where they wore the jerseys with black pants on September 16, 2005 against the Winnipeg Blue Bombers.
* [Ottawa Redblacks](/wiki/Ottawa_Redblacks "Ottawa Redblacks") – As part of the Signature series, the Redblacks wore red and plaid uniforms three times during the [2014 season](/wiki/2014_Ottawa_Redblacks_season "2014 Ottawa Redblacks season"), including an away game on November 7 against the Toronto Argonauts.[[48]](#cite_note-Signature-48) They wore them twice again in [2015](/wiki/2015_Ottawa_Redblacks_season "2015 Ottawa Redblacks season"). The team did not wear alternate uniforms again until [2021](/wiki/2021_Ottawa_Redblacks_season "2021 Ottawa Redblacks season") when they introduced new red alternate uniforms which were worn three times during the season.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The red uniforms were worn twice during the [2022 season](/wiki/2022_Ottawa_Redblacks_season "2022 Ottawa Redblacks season") and three times in [2023](/wiki/2023_Ottawa_Redblacks_season "2023 Ottawa Redblacks season") and [2024](/wiki/2024_Ottawa_Redblacks_season "2024 Ottawa Redblacks season").
* [Saskatchewan Roughriders](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Roughriders "Saskatchewan Roughriders") – First wore a black uniform with green numerals during the [2002 season](/wiki/2002_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2002 Saskatchewan Roughriders season"). The jersey was always worn with black pants and was last worn in [2006](/wiki/2006_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2006 Saskatchewan Roughriders season"). The team introduced retro-themed green alternate jerseys worn with silver pants in [2007](/wiki/2007_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2007 Saskatchewan Roughriders season"), based on the uniforms first worn in 1967. From [2010](/wiki/2010_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2010 Saskatchewan Roughriders season") to [2012](/wiki/2012_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2012 Saskatchewan Roughriders season"), these jerseys were worn with white pants. Also in 2010, white retro versions of these jerseys were introduced, but contrary to most CFL teams, the Roughriders continued to wear these until the 2012 season for select away games. In [2013](/wiki/2013_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2013 Saskatchewan Roughriders season"), the green retro jersey was worn with green pants. In [2014](/wiki/2014_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2014 Saskatchewan Roughriders season"), the team remodeled the retro green jersey, this time more resembling the uniforms from the early 1980s. Also in 2014, the team wore their Signature series uniforms twice, while also wearing "blitz green" for the first time.[[48]](#cite_note-Signature-48) Both the retro uniform and the Signature series uniforms were worn twice during 2015. The team introduced a modified version of their retro uniform in 2016 that did not have shoulder numbers and it has been worn at least twice each season since then, except for 2021 and 2022 when it was only worn for the Labour Day Classic. In addition to the retro uniforms, the Roughriders introduced obsidian alternate uniforms in [2024](/wiki/2024_Saskatchewan_Roughriders_season "2024 Saskatchewan Roughriders season") which featured a dark shade of green from helmets to socks and were worn three times, including for their home playoff game.[[69]](#cite_note-69)
* [Toronto Argonauts](/wiki/Toronto_Argonauts "Toronto Argonauts") – Were the last franchise to introduce a recurring alternate jersey, which they did in [2007](/wiki/2007_Toronto_Argonauts_season "2007 Toronto Argonauts season") on July 26 against the Montreal Alouettes. The jersey was Cambridge blue and partnered with their Oxford blue pants and was worn once in 2007, 2008 and 2009. With the retro uniforms that were introduced in [2009](/wiki/2009_Toronto_Argonauts_season "2009 Toronto Argonauts season"), the team also paired the retro Cambridge blue pants with the alternate jerseys for a monochrome Cambridge blue look that the team wore twice that year. The team wore an Oxford blue retro jersey in [2010](/wiki/2010_Toronto_Argonauts_season "2010 Toronto Argonauts season") that made a return appearance in [2011](/wiki/2011_Toronto_Argonauts_season "2011 Toronto Argonauts season").[[44]](#cite_note-argonauts.ca-44) The team became the first in the CFL to wear a white alternate uniform that wasn't based on a retro uniform when they wore their Signature series jerseys in [2014](/wiki/2014_Toronto_Argonauts_season "2014 Toronto Argonauts season"). This uniform was worn once at home and once away in both 2014 and 2015. No alternate jersey has been worn since 2016.
* [Winnipeg Blue Bombers](/wiki/Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers "Winnipeg Blue Bombers") – Wore gold alternate jerseys as part of their 75th anniversary season in [2005](/wiki/2005_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2005 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season"). The jerseys were worn with either white pants or blue pants and were worn every season through to [2010](/wiki/2010_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2010 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season"). Additionally, for the three seasons that the Blue Bombers qualified for the playoffs, the gold jerseys were worn for each game, both home and away, and in the [95th Grey Cup](/wiki/95th_Grey_Cup "95th Grey Cup").[[70]](#cite_note-70) Also in 2010, the club wore retro 1970s era royal blue jerseys, which were then adopted as the alternates for [2011](/wiki/2011_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2011 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season") and [2012](/wiki/2012_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2012 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season").[[71]](#cite_note-71) A modified 1980s era royal blue jersey was introduced in [2013](/wiki/2013_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2013 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season") and was also worn in [2014](/wiki/2014_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2014 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season"). The Blue Bombers only wore their [2014 Signature series uniforms](/wiki/2014_CFL_season#Signature_series "2014 CFL season") once, whereas all other teams wore theirs at least twice, possibly due to negative fan reception.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The team did not wear an alternate uniform until [2022](/wiki/2022_Winnipeg_Blue_Bombers_season "2022 Winnipeg Blue Bombers season") when they introduced all-blue uniforms with a white "W" on the front.[[73]](#cite_note-2022Bombers-73) These uniforms were worn twice in 2022 and 2023, including in the [Banjo Bowl](/wiki/Banjo_Bowl "Banjo Bowl") both years, but just once in 2024.[[73]](#cite_note-2022Bombers-73)

## Ice hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ice hockey")]

### National Hockey League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: National Hockey League")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Patrice_Bergeron_2011.jpg/250px-Patrice_Bergeron_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrice_Bergeron_2011.jpg)

[Patrice Bergeron](/wiki/Patrice_Bergeron "Patrice Bergeron") in all black [Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins") third jersey in 2011, with exchanged "spoked-B" and shield logo locations

In the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), each team has its own distinctive sweater design (ice hockey tradition usually refers to jerseys as "sweaters," because ice hockey players actually wore sweaters on the ice until the 1960s). Prior to 1995 (save a few isolated instances), each team only had two sweaters – one for home use, and one for the road. One sweater was dominantly white (or in a few instances, a light colour), and the other dominantly a dark colour. The home team has first choice of uniforms from the 1917–18 to the 1969–70 seasons (the white or yellow sweater was predominantly the road sweater with the dark sweater predominantly being the home sweater). In the 1970–1971 season, the NHL required teams to wear white (or yellow) sweaters at home and dark sweaters on the road; this rule remained in place until the end of the 2002–03 season. When the Third Sweater Program was introduced in the 1995–96 season, some teams wore the third sweater at home, which would have required an opponent to carry two sets of equipment and uniforms with them while on the road under most circumstances. This was alleviated starting in the 2003–04 season, when new rules mandated dark sweaters at home and white sweaters on the road, with yellow now being treated as a dark colour.

The first NHL team to feature a three-sweater rotation was the [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins"). From [1955](/wiki/1955%E2%80%9356_Boston_Bruins_season "1955–56 Boston Bruins season") through [1967](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_Boston_Bruins_season "1966–67 Boston Bruins season"), the Bruins had a gold home sweater and a white road sweater, and for several of those seasons featured a black third sweater. The [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") would later introduce a gold third sweater in [1981](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Pittsburgh_Penguins_season "1981–82 Pittsburgh Penguins season"), worn occasionally at home for two seasons for Sunday games and exclusively at home only in the [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_Pittsburgh_Penguins_season "1983–84 Pittsburgh Penguins season") season before retiring it. As part of the NHL's 75th anniversary in the [1991–92 season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_NHL_season "1991–92 NHL season"), for select games the [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six") teams wore throwback sweaters based on designs the teams wore at some point during the first 50 years of the league. The [Chicago Blackhawks](/wiki/Chicago_Blackhawks "Chicago Blackhawks") and [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings") wore their throwbacks again for a game at [Chicago Stadium](/wiki/Chicago_Stadium "Chicago Stadium") in the [1993–94 season](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season "1993–94 NHL season").

The 1995-96 third sweater program provided an opportunity for [CCM](/wiki/CCM_(ice_hockey) "CCM (ice hockey)") to further experiment with [dye-sublimation printing](/wiki/Dye-sublimation_printer "Dye-sublimation printer") on their sweaters. CCM had introduced the process to the NHL with the [1994 All-Star Game](/wiki/45th_National_Hockey_League_All-Star_Game "45th National Hockey League All-Star Game") sweaters and the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames")' new uniforms introduced in the [1994–95 season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Calgary_Flames_season "1994–95 Calgary Flames season"). The sublimated dye process allows for more complex stripe and graphic designs than were possible or practical with traditional manufacturing methods. The initial participating teams in the 1995–96 season embraced the concept, although the results were mixed. The Bruins' gold jersey, featuring a brown bear's head on the front and jagged striping, lasted through the 2005–06 season, while the Penguins' third jersey, featuring [gradient](/wiki/Gradient "Gradient") striping that complemented their logo at the time, replaced their road jerseys in 1997. The [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks")' red and black jersey with gradient striping, a subtle nod to their garish "Flying V" jerseys from 1978 to 1985, were retired in 1997 when the team changed colours and logos altogether. The jerseys worn by the [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") and [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings") only lasted one season. Due to the backlash against the less popular designs, the sublimation process was used by fewer teams in subsequent seasons, as newer thirds either rearranged the colours of an existing jersey, or experimented with different construction methods to create new designs.

With the advent of the [2003 Heritage Classic](/wiki/2003_Heritage_Classic "2003 Heritage Classic") outdoor game, the NHL introduced the Vintage jersey program, allowing a select number of teams to wear throwback designs for a limited number of games, in addition to their existing uniforms. These uniforms were designated with a special V-logo patch. Seven teams participated in the program, and included both home and road versions of throwback designs. As a result, the Bruins, Kings, Canucks, and [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") each had an unprecedented five jerseys in their rotation for that season. The program was suspended due to the [2004–05 NHL lockout](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lockout "2004–05 NHL lockout"), although the Vancouver Canucks and [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") would wear their vintage jerseys for a few games each in the 2005–06 season, and the Canucks and Bruins would wear their dark throwbacks as third jerseys (replacing sublimated-print third jerseys) in the 2006–07 season. The Canucks' and Bruins' throwbacks would later serve as the inspiration for their [RBK Edge](/wiki/NHL_uniform#Since_2007:_Reebok_Edge "NHL uniform") uniform redesigns in 2007.

Following a one-year hiatus on all third jerseys (save for the [2008 NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2008_NHL_Winter_Classic "2008 NHL Winter Classic"), where the Pittsburgh Penguins and [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") wore throwbacks for that game) as part of the NHL's transition to the Edge uniform system, the third jersey program returned in full earnest for the [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_NHL_season "2008–09 NHL season"). The Winter and Heritage Classics became showcases for throwback uniforms and new sweaters with a throwback aesthetic, while the [NHL Stadium Series](/wiki/NHL_Stadium_Series "NHL Stadium Series") became a showcase for more radical uniform designs. Third jerseys as a whole began to skew more to traditional stylings and throwback designs, especially for teams who more modernized looks in the Edge redesign. Another trend has been for teams to include an off-white cream colour, sometimes referred to as "vintage white" or "antique white", in place of pure white on their third or Classic jersey, attempting to replicate the discoloured appearance of a genuine aged sweater. This trend picked up after the [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild") began extensively using "Minnesota wheat", an official team colour similar to the vintage cream colour, in place of white trim on their dark jerseys.

The Detroit Red Wings were the last team in the NHL to introduce an alternate jersey. They added an alternate jersey in celebration of their 100th season for the 2025-26 season. The [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") have only had one season with an official alternate jersey; the 2006-07 season they used throwbacks of their 1944-47 white jerseys.

Prior to 2021, the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils") have eschewed the concept of third jerseys under general manager [Lou Lamoriello](/wiki/Lou_Lamoriello "Lou Lamoriello"). Instead, they wore special one-off alternates that were throwbacks of the classic red and green jerseys for one home game on or around [St. Patrick's Day](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Day "St. Patrick's Day") every season since 2009–10, with the exception of the [lockout-shortened](/wiki/2012-13_NHL_lockout "2012-13 NHL lockout") 2013 season, and have also worn them in their [2014 NHL Stadium Series](/wiki/2014_NHL_Stadium_Series "2014 NHL Stadium Series") outdoor game. However, in 2021, the Devils finally unveiled their first full-time third jersey, using black as the base color.

With [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") being replaced by parent company [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") as the NHL's uniform supplier for the [2017–18 NHL season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_NHL_season "2017–18 NHL season"), the third jersey program once again went on hiatus for one year.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Like what happened during the Reebok transition, alternate jerseys returned for the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_NHL_season "2018–19 NHL season").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

#### Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Usage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Joey_Hishon_in_Attack_3rd_Jersey.jpg/250px-Joey_Hishon_in_Attack_3rd_Jersey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joey_Hishon_in_Attack_3rd_Jersey.jpg)

[Owen Sound Attack](/wiki/Owen_Sound_Attack "Owen Sound Attack")'s Joey Hishon wearing a third uniform in the [Ontario Hockey League](/wiki/Ontario_Hockey_League "Ontario Hockey League")

Once a team has been granted permission by the league to use their new design, they will request and be allowed ten to fifteen games during the season in which they may use their third sweater. They may continue to use the third sweater in subsequent years as well. This alternate design allowed the team's appearance to flirt with radical designs which have occasionally gone on to become the new looks for some of the participating teams, though they can also be quite garish. An infamous example is the aforementioned St. Louis Blues' planned third sweater from 1996. The Mighty Ducks' third from that season, featuring mascot [Wildwing](/wiki/List_of_NHL_mascots#Wild_Wing "List of NHL mascots") bursting through a sheet of ice, was also considered particularly garish. Sales of third sweaters to fans have also provided significant additional income for cash-strapped NHL teams. For example, in 2013, the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames") introduced a third sweater in exactly the same colours as their primary home sweater, but with the addition of "western styling" mixed with traditional hockey sweater features such as laces and the team name in script on the front.[[76]](#cite_note-76) This doesn't always work; in the case of the Flames, they reverted to their throwback third jersey in the 2016–17 season.

Teams are also allowed to wear their third jerseys as often as desired in the [Stanley Cup Playoffs](/wiki/Stanley_Cup_Playoffs "Stanley Cup Playoffs"); however, they may only wear two different uniforms during the playoffs (one at home and one on the road). Examples of teams who wore third jerseys in the playoffs include the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") (in 1997), the [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars") (in 1999), the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") (from 2011 to 2014), the [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") (in 2014), the [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild") (from 2013 to 2017), the [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals") (in 2015), the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") (in 1999), the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") (in 2001 and 2009), the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") (in 1997, 2016, and 2021), the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers") (in 2017 and 2021), the Calgary Flames (in 2019), the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes") (in [2020](/wiki/Rogers_Place "Rogers Place")) and the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes") (in 2019, [2020](/wiki/Scotiabank_Arena "Scotiabank Arena"), and 2021). The Ducks, Stars, Flyers, Penguins, Oilers, Wild, Flames, and Coyotes are also examples of teams wearing third jerseys in the playoffs that are then promoted to full-time jerseys the following season.

#### Chronology

Several teams have had multiple designs of their third jersey.

* 1966–67: *[Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs")* (inspired by Canada's Centennial celebrations in 1967, the Leafs had a completely different uniform set for that year's Stanley Cup playoffs, which culminated with a Stanley Cup victory. The modified Leafs uniform featured a new Maple Leaf emblem, replacing the traditional maple leaf with a design imitating the leaf found on Canada's new Maple Leaf Flag introduced in 1965. This uniform was adopted by the team the following season (1967–68) and remained until a completely new uniform design was unveiled for the 1970–71 season.)
* 1981–82: *[Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins")* (remained until 1984–1985 season)
* 1991–92: *[Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins")*, *[Chicago Blackhawks](/wiki/Chicago_Blackhawks "Chicago Blackhawks")*, *[Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings")*, *[Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens")*, *[New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers")*, *[Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs")* (all as throwback/vintage jerseys)

* 1995–96: *[Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks")*, *[Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins")* (through 2005–06), *[Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings")*, *[Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins")* (through 1996–97; would eventually become their regular road jersey), *[Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks")* (through 1996–97)

On the shoulder of the jersey, there is a patch of the Mighty Duck logo which was no longer used in the [2006–07 NHL season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NHL_season "2006–07 NHL season") with the logo the Ducks are currently using.

The [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers") introduced a new throwback alternate orange jersey, while the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") added a black throwback jersey that was the team's road uniform from 1989 to 1997. The [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals") added a red alternate jersey based on the white alternate jersey they used from the 2011–12 to the 2014–15 seasons. In addition, the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche") and the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") changed their alternate jerseys. The two-tone third jersey worn by the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") was permanently discontinued; in its place, a "third jersey" identical to the white away jersey was used for six home games (technically it *was* the same jersey, but as previously noted it required special dispensation from the league to allow it).[[4]](#cite_note-sabreswhiteathome-4)
* 2016–17: The Pittsburgh Penguins' third jersey that was worn during the 2014–15 and 2015–16 seasons was promoted to regular use at home; while the Boston Bruins replaced their third jersey with the jersey that was first worn at the [2016 NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2016_NHL_Winter_Classic "2016 NHL Winter Classic"). The Los Angeles Kings and Philadelphia Flyers introduced special third jerseys, while the St. Louis Blues removed their third jerseys.
* 2017–18: No teams were allowed to have regular alternate jerseys due to the introduction of the Adidas jersey style. However, teams participating in outdoor games (the [NHL 100 Classic](/wiki/NHL_100_Classic "NHL 100 Classic"), [Winter Classic](/wiki/2018_NHL_Winter_Classic "2018 NHL Winter Classic"), and [Stadium Series](/wiki/2018_NHL_Stadium_Series "2018 NHL Stadium Series") games) had special uniforms for those games. In addition, the Toronto Maple Leafs wore a [Toronto Arenas](/wiki/Toronto_Arenas "Toronto Arenas") throwback for the Next Century Game, commemorating the 100th anniversary of the first game in NHL history.
* 2018–19: The following teams announced the use of third jerseys for the 2018–19: the [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks"), the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes"), the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames"), the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes"), the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche"), the [Columbus Blue Jackets](/wiki/Columbus_Blue_Jackets "Columbus Blue Jackets"), the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers"), the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils"), the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders"), the [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators"), the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers"), the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins"), the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks"), the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues"), the [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals") and the [Winnipeg Jets](/wiki/Winnipeg_Jets "Winnipeg Jets"). The [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins") and [Chicago Blackhawks](/wiki/Chicago_Blackhawks "Chicago Blackhawks") wore throwback jerseys during the [2019 NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2019_NHL_Winter_Classic "2019 NHL Winter Classic"). The [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes") announced that they would wear green [Hartford Whalers](/wiki/Hartford_Whalers "Hartford Whalers") jerseys for select games against the [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins") during the 2018–19 season.
* 2019–20: The [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins"), the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers"), and the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") added new third jerseys. Special throwback uniform designs were worn by the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres"), the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames"), the [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars"), the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), the [Nashville Predators](/wiki/Nashville_Predators "Nashville Predators"), and the [Winnipeg Jets](/wiki/Winnipeg_Jets "Winnipeg Jets") for a few games during the season.
* 2020–21: NHL began partnering with Adidas to introduce the *Reverse Retro* program, where all 31 teams are issued special uniforms which are based on throwback jerseys that were worn during previous seasons. Several teams, including the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes"), the [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins"), the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes"), the [Columbus Blue Jackets](/wiki/Columbus_Blue_Jackets "Columbus Blue Jackets"), the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), the [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild"), the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens"), the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils"), the [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks"), and the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues"), received uniforms that swapped the colour palettes. The introduction of the *Reverse Retro* jerseys marks the first time in history that all 31 teams have at least three jerseys. In addition to the introduction of the *Reverse Retro* series, the [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars"), the [Vegas Golden Knights](/wiki/Vegas_Golden_Knights "Vegas Golden Knights"), and the [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals") introduced new fourth jerseys, while the throwback uniform of the [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames") was promoted to a home uniform and the old home uniform was relegated to a fourth jersey; the third jerseys of the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") and the [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators") were redesigned into *Reverse Retro* jerseys as part of those teams introducing new wholesale uniform sets.[[78]](#cite_note-78)
* 2021–22: New third jerseys were unveiled for the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils"), [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins"), [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs"), and [Winnipeg Jets](/wiki/Winnipeg_Jets "Winnipeg Jets"). In addition, the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes")' previous "coyote head" home jersey was relegated to a third jersey.
* 2022–23: A second set of *Reverse Retro* jerseys were introduced. The [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres"), [Calgary Flames](/wiki/Calgary_Flames "Calgary Flames"), [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes"), and [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") unveiled modernized versions of 1990s-era throwback jerseys, while the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes") unveiled a new red alternate jersey.
* 2023–24: The [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") and [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins") wore anniversary-specific third jerseys for the season. The [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild") brought back their [Minnesota North Stars](/wiki/Minnesota_North_Stars "Minnesota North Stars")-inspired green and gold "Reverse Retro" jersey as an alternate, and the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers"), [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") and [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning") unveiled new alternate jerseys.

## Rugby league

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rugby league")]

### National Rugby League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Third_jersey&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: National Rugby League")]

In recent years the third jersey has appeared in the Australian [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL"), with every team having a 'home' jersey, an 'away' jersey and a 'heritage' jersey. The NRL does not currently require third or alternate jerseys, because most clashes can be resolved with away jerseys or using modified under-20s jerseys.

* [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") used a white jersey (the under-20s away jersey) against Canberra in 2008, even though the NRL stated that the Souths and Canberra jerseys don't clash. They have a similar jersey for 2009 which contains tribal Aboriginal and Maori designs.
* [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") have adopted a home (yellow with blue designs), away (blue with yellow designs, however in 2007 this was the alternate) and alternate (white with yellow designs, however in 2007 was away) jersey scheme. They (along with Manly-Warringah and Illawarra) were one of the pioneers of away jerseys in rugby league in Australia.
* [Canterbury Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury_Bulldogs "Canterbury Bulldogs") use a jersey reminiscent of their training jersey as a clash strip (also their under-20s away jersey). They also wear their Berries strip from the 1960s as a heritage strip and a similar (yet quite different) jersey (white with blue and black butcher stripes) for trials.
* [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") have been known to wear a blue and aqua alternate jersey, however, it is rare to see as blue has become a colour hated by Queensland rugby league fans.
* [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") have worn a white version of their 2008 home jersey, switching the white/orange areas, and introduced a "10 Year Anniversary" jersey, which is white with black and orange V's. They also wear modernised versions of the old [Balmain Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") and [Western Suburbs Magpies](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Western Suburbs Magpies") strips from the 1980s.
* Though not technically third jumpers, the Centenary of Rugby League celebrations left all teams with special jerseys for the Centenary round, and in 2009 several teams wore the same jerseys (the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights"), however, wore their foundation strip, in 2008 they wore a [Newcastle Rebels](/wiki/Newcastle_Rebels "Newcastle Rebels") jersey, which was the first Newcastle team in the NSWRL and played in the NSWRL's foundation season. The [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") wore a jersey symbolising the history of rugby league in Auckland, with a dark blue jersey with 2 white V's, while the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") wore their 2000's light purple with white lightning bolts away strip in the 2009 Heritage Round, while in 2008 they wore their foundation jersey with V's).
* As a result of the above point, the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") have used five jerseys in 2008.
